source_text: When we were children, there were many cats in our garden.

reference_translations.text: Quando eravamo bambini, c'erano molti gatti nel nostro giardino.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect for both the childhood frame (eravamo) and the existential past description (c'erano).

reference_translations.text: Da bambini, c'erano molti gatti nel nostro giardino.
reference_translations.notes: Natural alternative with 'da bambini' for 'when we were children'.

reference_translations.text: Quando siamo stati bambini, ci sono stati molti gatti...
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: defaults to passato prossimo for both states. The miss is using PP for past states/durations rather than imperfect.

explanation: Both clauses describe past states, so Italian uses the imperfect indicative for each. 'Were children' takes eravamo (1pl of essere); 'there were' takes the plural existential c'erano (3pl of essere with locative ci). Note the articulated preposition nel (in + il).

source_text: The house was big and the rooms were full of light.

reference_translations.text: La casa era grande e le stanze erano piene di luce.
reference_translations.notes: Standard description. Imperfect 3sg (era) for the singular subject, 3pl (erano) for the plural.

reference_translations.text: La casa era grande e le camere erano piene di luce.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'camera' is interchangeable with 'stanza' for 'room' in most contexts.

explanation: Both clauses describe how things were, so Italian uses the imperfect indicative for both. Essere 3sg era for the singular subject, 3pl erano for the plural. The agreement of 'piene' is f.pl to match 'le stanze'.

source_text: Quando vivevamo in Toscana, bevevamo il vino della casa a ogni cena.

reference_translations.text: When we lived in Tuscany, we used to drink the house wine at every dinner.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Used to drink' captures the habitual reading of bevevamo.

reference_translations.text: When we were living in Tuscany, we would drink the house wine at every dinner.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'would' for habitual past is equally natural here.

reference_translations.text: When we lived in Tuscany, we drank the house wine at every dinner.
reference_translations.notes: Acceptable: bare simple past 'drank' is unmarked English for a habit, though it loses the explicit habitual reading.

reference_translations.text: When we have lived in Tuscany, we have drunk the house wine...
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: English present perfect doesn't fit a definite past period. Tense-mapping error.

explanation: Italian's imperfect indicative covers both English's past habitual ('used to', 'would') and bare simple past for repeated actions. Here 'a ogni cena' (at every dinner) signals habitual, so 'used to drink' or 'would drink' are the most natural renderings; 'drank' is also acceptable. Note bevevamo from bere (stem-expansion: bev-) and vivevamo from vivere (regular -ere).
examiner_note: Marker should credit any of the three habitual renderings. Penalise present perfect ('have drunk') which doesn't fit a closed past period.

source_text: Grandpa always used to say the same things at the dinner table.

reference_translations.text: Il nonno diceva sempre le stesse cose a tavola.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect 3sg of dire (diceva) for the habitual past; 'a tavola' for 'at the table' / 'at mealtimes'.

reference_translations.text: Il nonno diceva sempre le stesse cose a cena.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'a cena' (at dinner) narrows from 'at the table' to specifically dinner.

reference_translations.text: Nonno ha sempre detto le stesse cose a tavola.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: passato prossimo doesn't fit the habitual reading. PP would mean 'has always said' as a present-perfect summary, not 'used to say' as a habit.

translation.reveal: table - tavola / tavolo (a tavola = at the (dining) table)

explanation: 'Always used to say' is a habitual past in English, mapped directly to Italian imperfect indicative. The 3sg of dire in the imperfect is diceva (expanded stem dic- + -ev- + -a). The fixed expression 'a tavola' refers to the dining table as a setting, distinct from 'al tavolo' which would specify a particular table.

source_text: On Sundays we used to make a long walk in the countryside.

reference_translations.text: La domenica facevamo una lunga passeggiata in campagna.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'La domenica' (with definite article) for 'on Sundays' (every Sunday); facevamo from fare; 'fare una passeggiata' is the idiomatic collocation.

reference_translations.text: Di domenica facevamo una lunga passeggiata in campagna.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'di domenica' is interchangeable with 'la domenica' for habitual 'on Sundays'.

reference_translations.text: Le domeniche abbiamo fatto una lunga passeggiata in campagna.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: PP (abbiamo fatto) for a habitual action over an open past period. Should be imperfect.

explanation: 'On Sundays... used to make' is a habit, so Italian uses the imperfect indicative. The 1pl of fare in the imperfect is facevamo (expanded stem fac- + -ev- + -amo). The idiom is 'fare una passeggiata' (literally 'to make a walk'); English 'take a walk' becomes 'fare' in Italian, not 'prendere'.

source_text: When I was young, I used to play tennis every weekend.

reference_translations.text: Da giovane, giocavo a tennis ogni weekend.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Da giovane' for 'when I was young'; giocavo for habitual past; 'giocare a' for 'to play (a sport)'.

reference_translations.text: Quando ero giovane, giocavo a tennis ogni fine settimana.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'quando ero giovane' is interchangeable with 'da giovane'; 'fine settimana' is the native Italian for 'weekend'.

reference_translations.text: Quando sono stato giovane, ho giocato a tennis ogni weekend.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: both PPs ('sono stato giovane', 'ho giocato') for a habitual past frame. Should be imperfect throughout.

explanation: 'Used to play' is the English habitual past, mapped directly to Italian imperfect indicative. The 1sg of giocare is giocavo; the 'a' is required when 'giocare' is followed by a sport (giocare a calcio, giocare a tennis).

source_text: Every Saturday they would go to the market early in the morning.

reference_translations.text: Ogni sabato andavano al mercato presto la mattina.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Would go' as habitual past = imperfect andavano.

reference_translations.text: Tutti i sabati andavano al mercato presto la mattina.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'tutti i sabati' (every Saturday) is interchangeable with 'ogni sabato'.

reference_translations.text: Il sabato andavano al mercato presto la mattina.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'il sabato' (with article) signals habitual 'on Saturdays' in Italian.

explanation: English habitual 'would' (when it doesn't mean conditional 'would') maps to Italian imperfect. The 3pl of andare in the imperfect is andavano. Note the articulated preposition al = a + il.

source_text: Mio nonno fumava la pipa sul balcone dopo cena.

reference_translations.text: My grandfather used to smoke a pipe on the balcony after dinner.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Used to smoke' captures the implicit habitual reading of fumava.

reference_translations.text: My grandfather would smoke a pipe on the balcony after dinner.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'would smoke' for habitual past.

reference_translations.text: My grandfather smoked a pipe on the balcony after dinner.
reference_translations.notes: Acceptable: bare simple past 'smoked' is unmarked English for a habit.

reference_translations.text: My grandfather has smoked a pipe on the balcony after dinner.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: present perfect doesn't fit a closed past habit.

explanation: There's no explicit adverb here, but 'sul balcone dopo cena' reads as a recurring scene rather than a one-off event, so the imperfect's habitual reading is the natural one. English allows 'used to smoke', 'would smoke', or bare 'smoked' for the same idea.
examiner_note: Marker accepts any of the three habitual renderings. The diagnostic is reading the implicit habitual from context.

source_text: It was raining and the streets were empty.

reference_translations.text: Pioveva e le strade erano vuote.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Two parallel imperfects: pioveva (weather state), erano (descriptive state).

reference_translations.text: Pioveva e per le strade non c'era nessuno.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'non c'era nessuno' (there was nobody) for 'were empty'.

explanation: Both clauses are past states (weather, descriptive condition). Italian uses the imperfect indicative for both. Pioveva is the impersonal 3sg of piovere; erano is the 3pl of essere agreeing with le strade.

source_text: She was very tall and had short dark hair.

reference_translations.text: Era molto alta e aveva i capelli corti e scuri.
reference_translations.notes: Standard physical description. Two imperfects for two parallel past states.

reference_translations.text: Lei era molto alta e aveva i capelli corti e scuri.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: optional subject pronoun lei for emphasis or disambiguation.

reference_translations.text: È stata molto alta e ha avuto i capelli corti e scuri.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: PP for both states. 'Ha avuto i capelli' would suggest a discrete event of having hair, which is nonsensical.

explanation: Descriptive past states take the imperfect indicative: era (3sg essere, agreeing in gender — alta f.sg — with the female subject), aveva (3sg avere). Italian 'avere i capelli + adjective' is the standard 'to have X hair' construction.

source_text: Il bambino dormiva tranquillamente nel suo lettino.

reference_translations.text: The child was sleeping peacefully in his cot.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect dormiva → past progressive 'was sleeping' for the in-progress reading.

reference_translations.text: The child was sleeping quietly in his little bed.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'quietly' for tranquillamente; 'little bed' for lettino (diminutive).

reference_translations.text: The child slept peacefully in his cot.
reference_translations.notes: Acceptable: bare simple past works but loses the explicit progressive reading.

translation.reveal: lettino - little bed / cot (diminutive of letto)

explanation: The imperfect dormiva describes an ongoing past state. English's natural rendering is past progressive 'was sleeping'; bare 'slept' also works but is less explicit about the in-progress reading.

source_text: While I was watching TV, the phone rang.

reference_translations.text: Mentre guardavo la TV, è suonato il telefono.
reference_translations.notes: Standard mentre + imperfect, + PP-event.

reference_translations.text: Mentre stavo guardando la TV, è suonato il telefono.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'stavo guardando' (stare + gerund) is a more explicit progressive.

reference_translations.text: Mentre ho guardato la TV, è suonato il telefono.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: PP in the mentre-clause. The mentre-clause must be imperfect because it sets the background.

auxiliary.reveal: essere (when intransitive: il telefono è suonato)

explanation: The mentre-clause sets the ongoing background ('was watching TV') and takes the imperfect indicative. The main clause names the punctuating event ('the phone rang') and takes the passato prossimo. Note that suonare here is intransitive (the phone made a sound) and idiomatically takes essere: è suonato il telefono.
examiner_note: Suonare's auxiliary can vary (essere for ringing on its own, avere when someone rings something). In this 'the phone rang' context, essere is the natural choice.

source_text: It was raining when we arrived at the hotel.

reference_translations.text: Pioveva quando siamo arrivati in albergo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect for weather background, PP for the arrival event.

reference_translations.text: Pioveva quando siamo arrivati all'hotel.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'hotel' is accepted alongside 'albergo'.

explanation: Weather state (pioveva) sets the background; arrival (siamo arrivati) is the foregrounded event. Italian uses imperfect for the state and passato prossimo for the event. Arrivare takes essere as a motion-intransitive, with participle agreement on the subject (1pl: siamo arrivati).

source_text: I was reading a book when suddenly the lights went out.

reference_translations.text: Leggevo un libro quando all'improvviso è andata via la luce.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect leggevo + the idiomatic 'è andata via la luce' (the light went away).

reference_translations.text: Stavo leggendo un libro quando improvvisamente è saltata la corrente.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: stare + gerund for emphatic progressive; 'è saltata la corrente' is another standard Italian way of saying the power went out.

reference_translations.text: Leggevo un libro quando improvvisamente sono andate via le luci.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: plural 'le luci' (the lights) with f.pl agreement on the participle (andate).

translation.reveal: light - luce (idiom: la luce = electricity, often singular)
(lights) go out - andare via (la luce) / saltare la corrente

explanation: Reading is the ongoing background (imperfect leggevo); the lights-going-out is the foregrounded event (PP è andata via). Italian has two idioms here: 'è andata via la luce' (the electricity went away) and 'è saltata la corrente' (the current jumped). Both take essere, with agreement on the feminine subject.
examiner_note: Marker should accept either Italian idiom; both render English 'the lights went out' naturally.

source_text: Mentre cenavamo, qualcuno ha bussato alla porta.

reference_translations.text: While we were having dinner, someone knocked at the door.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect cenavamo → past progressive 'were having dinner'; PP ha bussato → simple past 'knocked'.

reference_translations.text: While we were eating, someone knocked on the door.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'were eating' for cenavamo (literally 'were dining'); 'on the door' for 'alla porta'.

reference_translations.text: As we were having dinner, there was a knock at the door.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'as' for 'mentre'; 'there was a knock' rephrases the event.

translation.reveal: cenare - to have dinner / to dine

infinitive.reveal: cenavamo from cenare - to have dinner

explanation: The mentre-clause is the ongoing background (imperfect → English past progressive); the main clause is the foregrounded event (PP → English simple past). 'Mentre cenavamo, qualcuno ha bussato' = 'While we were having dinner, someone knocked'.

source_text: We had been waiting for two hours when the train finally arrived.

reference_translations.text: Aspettavamo da due ore quando finalmente è arrivato il treno.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect + da + time-phrase for English past perfect continuous.

reference_translations.text: Aspettavamo il treno da due ore quando finalmente è arrivato.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'aspettare' fronted with direct object 'il treno', then the arrival as a bare verb.

reference_translations.text: Abbiamo aspettato per due ore quando il treno è finalmente arrivato.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: PP with 'per' doesn't capture the 'had been waiting' duration-up-to-event reading. Italian uses imperfect + da for this.

explanation: English past perfect continuous 'had been waiting for X hours' maps to Italian imperfect + da + time-phrase: aspettavamo da due ore. The arrival is the foregrounded event in the PP (è arrivato). Italian doesn't use 'per' here; that would shift the reading to a bounded duration (we waited for two hours then stopped).
examiner_note: The duration construction is one of the highest-payoff bits of imperfect knowledge; English speakers consistently default to PP + per, which produces a different reading.

source_text: Studiavo italiano da sei mesi quando sono andato in Italia per la prima volta.

reference_translations.text: I had been studying Italian for six months when I went to Italy for the first time.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect + da → past perfect continuous.

reference_translations.text: I had studied Italian for six months when I went to Italy for the first time.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: bare past perfect 'had studied' also works, less progressive flavour.

explanation: The reverse direction: Italian imperfect + da → English past perfect continuous. 'Studiavo italiano da sei mesi' = 'I had been studying Italian for six months'. The 'when' clause anchors the reference point (the going-to-Italy event).

source_text: I was twelve years old when my family moved to Milan.

reference_translations.text: Avevo dodici anni quando la mia famiglia si è trasferita a Milano.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Avere X anni' for age, imperfect avevo for past age.

reference_translations.text: Avevo dodici anni quando ci siamo trasferiti a Milano.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'ci siamo trasferiti' (we moved) — common rephrasing if the speaker includes themselves.

reference_translations.text: Sono stato dodici anni quando la mia famiglia si è trasferita a Milano.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: 'sono stato dodici anni' uses essere instead of avere for age. Italian uses 'avere X anni'.

reference_translations.text: Ho avuto dodici anni quando la mia famiglia si è trasferita a Milano.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: PP 'ho avuto X anni' would mean 'I had X years' as an event, which is nonsensical. Age in the past takes imperfect.

translation.reveal: to be X years old - avere X anni

explanation: Two distinct misses to dodge here. First, Italian uses 'avere' for age, not 'essere' ('avevo dodici anni', not 'ero dodici anni'). Second, age in the past is a state, so it takes the imperfect indicative, not the passato prossimo. The moving-to-Milan event correctly takes PP.
examiner_note: Hot-spot item: combines two common English-influenced misses. Both negative references are worth tracking.

source_text: It was three o'clock in the afternoon and very hot.

reference_translations.text: Erano le tre del pomeriggio e faceva molto caldo.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Erano le tre' for time (plural agreement); 'faceva caldo' for weather (imperfect of fare).

reference_translations.text: Erano le tre di pomeriggio e faceva molto caldo.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'di pomeriggio' without article is also heard.

translation.reveal: (it was) X o'clock - erano le X / era l'una / era mezzanotte
hot - caldo (weather: fare caldo = to be hot)

explanation: Two age_time_weather idioms in one sentence: 'erano le tre' for time of day (plural verb agreeing with plural hours) and 'faceva caldo' for weather (idiom with fare, which here uses the expanded stem fac- + ev + a = faceva). Both take the imperfect indicative because they describe past states, not events.

source_text: It was snowing heavily and we couldn't see anything.

reference_translations.text: Nevicava forte e non si vedeva nulla.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Nevicava (weather imperfect); 'non si vedeva nulla' (impersonal 'one couldn't see anything').

reference_translations.text: Nevicava forte e non riuscivamo a vedere niente.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'non riuscivamo a vedere' (we couldn't manage to see) for an explicit 1pl subject.

explanation: Both clauses describe past states: weather (nevicava) and the resulting condition of not being able to see (non si vedeva). Italian uses the imperfect indicative for both. 'Si vedeva' is the impersonal-si construction, equivalent to English 'one could see' or 'you could see'.

source_text: Quando mia madre aveva vent'anni, lavorava in una libreria del centro.

reference_translations.text: When my mother was twenty, she worked in a bookshop in the city centre.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Was twenty' for aveva vent'anni; 'worked' (simple past) for habitual lavorava.

reference_translations.text: When my mother was twenty years old, she used to work in a bookshop downtown.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: explicit 'twenty years old' and 'used to work' for the habit.

reference_translations.text: When my mother had twenty years, she has worked in a bookshop in the centre.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: literal 'had twenty years' (Italian → English calque); 'has worked' present perfect with a closed past frame.

translation.reveal: avere X anni - to be X years old

explanation: The two imperfects in the Italian both shift to English past forms. 'Aveva vent'anni' → 'was twenty' (age, English uses essere-equivalent); 'lavorava' → 'worked' or 'used to work' (habit). Note the false friend: libreria is a bookshop in Italian, not a library (which is biblioteca).
examiner_note: Two anti-misses worth catching: 'had twenty years' (literal calque) and 'library' (false friend).

source_text: I'd like an espresso, please.

reference_translations.text: Volevo un espresso, per favore.
reference_translations.notes: Standard polite-present imperfect of volere.

reference_translations.text: Vorrei un espresso, per favore.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: conditional vorrei is equally polite and very common; many speakers prefer it in writing.

explanation: English 'I'd like' is a polite request. Italian has two natural renderings: the polite-present imperfect 'volevo' (literally 'I wanted') and the present conditional 'vorrei' (literally 'I would want'). Both are polite; in service contexts (cafés, shops), volevo is the colloquial choice and vorrei the slightly more formal one.
examiner_note: Both are correct. The bucket attribution is to imperfect.usage.polite when volevo is used; this item also lives in the conditional tree for vorrei.

source_text: Excuse me, I wanted to ask you a question.

reference_translations.text: Scusi, volevo farLe una domanda.
reference_translations.notes: Standard formal. Polite-present imperfect volevo + formal LE (3sg) indirect object.

reference_translations.text: Scusa, volevo farti una domanda.
reference_translations.notes: Informal variant: scusa (tu) + farti (informal indirect object).

reference_translations.text: Scusi, vorrei farLe una domanda.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: vorrei (conditional) is interchangeable with volevo here.

reference_translations.text: Scusi, ho voluto farLe una domanda.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: PP 'ho voluto' means 'I insisted on' (modal-discrimination meaning), not 'I wanted (politely)'. The polite-present requires the imperfect.

translation.reveal: to ask (a question) - fare una domanda

explanation: Polite-present imperfect: 'volevo farLe una domanda' is the standard polite way to introduce a question. The conditional vorrei is interchangeable. Note that this is a place where the imperfect/PP modal discrimination matters: 'ho voluto farLe una domanda' would shift to 'I insisted on asking you' (the strong-will reading of volere in PP), which is rude in a polite request.
examiner_note: Borderline with discrimination.modals.volere. The polite-present reading is the dominant one here; the modal-discrimination reading is the contrast.

source_text: When we were students, we ate at the canteen every day.

reference_translations.text: Quando eravamo studenti, mangiavamo in mensa tutti i giorni.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Both imperfects for sustained past period + habit.

reference_translations.text: Da studenti, mangiavamo in mensa ogni giorno.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'da studenti' for 'when we were students'.

reference_translations.text: Quando siamo stati studenti, abbiamo mangiato in mensa tutti i giorni.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: PPs for both states. Closed-bounded period would need different framing.

explanation: 'When we were students' frames a sustained past period; 'every day' makes the eating habitual within it. Italian uses imperfect for both. Eravamo + mangiavamo.

source_text: Yesterday I felt very tired and went to bed early.

reference_translations.text: Ieri mi sentivo molto stanco e sono andato a letto presto.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect for the state of tiredness; PP for the going-to-bed event.

reference_translations.text: Ieri mi sentivo molto stanca e sono andata a letto presto.
reference_translations.notes: Feminine variant.

reference_translations.text: Ieri mi sono sentito molto stanco e sono andato a letto presto.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: PP 'mi sono sentito' shifts the reading to a discrete moment of feeling (e.g. 'I came to feel'). Less natural but defensible.

explanation: Despite 'ieri' being a PP cue, the feeling-tired is a state and the going-to-bed is an event. Italian splits the tenses accordingly: imperfect for the state (mi sentivo) and PP for the event (sono andato).
examiner_note: Mixed-aspect sentence under a single time-anchor (ieri). Diagnostic of whether the learner reads aspect rather than just the adverb.

source_text: I knew Maria for years, but I only met her sister at the wedding.

reference_translations.text: Conoscevo Maria da anni, ma ho conosciuto sua sorella solo al matrimonio.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect conoscevo = was acquainted; PP ho conosciuto = met for the first time.

translation.reveal: to meet (for the first time) - conoscere (in PP: ho conosciuto)

explanation: English 'knew' and 'met' map to the imperfect and PP of conoscere respectively. The sentence frames both contrasts cleanly: 'for years' triggers conoscevo (state of acquaintance); 'only at the wedding' triggers ho conosciuto (first-meeting event).
examiner_note: Doubly diagnostic: tests both the conoscere shift and the imperfect-duration with 'da anni'.

source_text: Da ragazzino guardavo i cartoni animati ogni sabato mattina.

reference_translations.text: When I was a kid, I used to watch cartoons every Saturday morning.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Used to watch' for habitual past.

reference_translations.text: When I was a kid, I would watch cartoons every Saturday morning.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'would' for habitual past.

reference_translations.text: As a kid, I watched cartoons every Saturday morning.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: bare past 'watched' is acceptable for a habit.

explanation: Italian imperfect for childhood habit → English 'used to / would' or bare simple past. Marker should credit any of the three habitual renderings.

source_text: Sono andato in palestra solo una volta lo scorso mese.

reference_translations.text: I went to the gym only once last month.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. PP for the single past occasion.

reference_translations.text: I went to the gym just once last month.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'just once' for 'solo una volta'.

explanation: PP 'sono andato' for a single past occasion with a discrete count ('solo una volta'). The natural English rendering uses simple past.
examiner_note: Counter-example to the imperfect: the marker fires the discrimination bucket because the sentence is in the imperfect/PP contrast zone but the right choice here is PP. Negative example item.

source_text: We found out about the accident only this morning.

reference_translations.text: Abbiamo saputo dell'incidente solo questa mattina.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. PP of sapere = found out.

reference_translations.text: Solo questa mattina abbiamo saputo dell'incidente.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: fronted time-adverb.

translation.reveal: found out - PP of sapere (abbiamo saputo)

explanation: 'Found out' is the textbook English for the PP of sapere. 'Solo questa mattina' pinpoints the moment of learning. Abbiamo saputo + dell'incidente (di + l'incidente articulated).

source_text: Sapevo che non era una buona idea, ma ho accettato comunque.

reference_translations.text: I knew it wasn't a good idea, but I accepted anyway.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect sapevo = knew (state); PP ho accettato for the discrete decision.

reference_translations.text: I knew it was a bad idea, but I accepted anyway.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'a bad idea' rephrases 'non era una buona idea'.

translation.reveal: sapere - to know (sapevo = I knew, state)

explanation: Sapevo = ongoing state of knowing → 'knew' in English. The PP 'ho accettato' for the discrete decision-event.

source_text: I had known him for years before he told me his real name.

reference_translations.text: Lo conoscevo da anni prima che mi dicesse il suo vero nome.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect conoscevo + da anni for English past perfect 'had known'. Subjunctive 'dicesse' in the prima che clause.

reference_translations.text: Lo conoscevo da anni prima che mi dicesse come si chiamava davvero.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: paraphrase of 'real name'.

translation.reveal: real name - vero nome / nome vero

explanation: English 'had known' is the past perfect equivalent of Italian's imperfect + da construction. 'Lo conoscevo da anni' = 'I had known him for years'. The 'prima che' clause triggers the subjunctive (dicesse).
examiner_note: Touches conoscere modal-discrimination + duration + subjunctive in 'prima che'. Rich item.

source_text: I had to work all weekend to finish the report.

reference_translations.text: Ho dovuto lavorare tutto il weekend per finire la relazione.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. PP of dovere = had to (and did). Avere as auxiliary (lavorare is avere-taking).

reference_translations.text: Ho dovuto lavorare tutto il fine settimana per finire la relazione.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'fine settimana' for 'weekend'.

translation.reveal: had to - PP of dovere (ho dovuto)

explanation: English 'had to' with implied completion → PP of dovere in Italian. Ho dovuto lavorare per finire = had to work to finish.

source_text: Dovevo studiare ma sono uscito con gli amici.

reference_translations.text: I was supposed to study but I went out with friends.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect dovevo with explicit non-fulfilment.

reference_translations.text: I should have studied but I went out with friends.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'should have' for the unfulfilled obligation reading.

reference_translations.text: I had to study but I went out with friends.
reference_translations.notes: MARGINAL: 'had to' implies obligation followed through; doesn't quite fit the unfulfilled reading. The right rendering is 'was supposed to'.

translation.reveal: dovere - must / have to (dovevo = was supposed to)

explanation: The imperfect dovevo with the 'ma sono uscito' clause makes it clear the obligation wasn't met. English 'was supposed to' is the closest natural rendering. 'Had to' is more aligned with the PP and would mismatch the meaning here.
examiner_note: Negative reference flags the 'had to' as a likely English-speaker miss.

source_text: We were supposed to leave at six, but we missed the alarm.

reference_translations.text: Dovevamo partire alle sei, ma non abbiamo sentito la sveglia.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect dovevamo = were supposed to. 'Non abbiamo sentito la sveglia' = literally 'we didn't hear the alarm'.

reference_translations.text: Dovevamo partire alle sei, ma abbiamo perso la sveglia.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'abbiamo perso la sveglia' (we missed the alarm).

translation.reveal: to be supposed to - imperfect of dovere (dovevamo)

explanation: English 'were supposed to' maps directly to the imperfect of dovere. The follow-up clause confirms non-fulfilment, anchoring the imperfect reading.

source_text: After much effort, I finally managed to talk to him.

reference_translations.text: Dopo molti sforzi, finalmente sono riuscito a parlargli.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Riuscire a' is the natural Italian for 'manage to'. PP for the breakthrough event.

reference_translations.text: Dopo molti sforzi, finalmente ho potuto parlargli.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: PP of potere also carries the 'managed to' reading.

translation.reveal: to manage to - riuscire a / PP of potere (ho potuto)

explanation: Two natural Italian renderings for English 'managed to': 'sono riuscito a' (the most common) or 'ho potuto' (the modal-discrimination PP reading). Both are accepted; the second is what this item specifically tests.
examiner_note: Both forms count for the discrimination bucket. The dispatch's hot-spot is the potere PP reading.

source_text: Da giovane potevo lavorare dodici ore di fila senza problemi.

reference_translations.text: When I was young, I could work twelve hours straight without any problem.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect potevo = was able to (sustained ability).

reference_translations.text: When I was young, I was able to work twelve hours in a row with no problem.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'was able to' for potevo.

translation.reveal: potere - can / be able to (potevo = was able to / could)
di fila - in a row / straight (consecutive)

explanation: Sustained past ability → imperfect potevo → English 'could' or 'was able to'. Both natural in English.

source_text: Mum could speak three languages fluently when she was twenty.

reference_translations.text: La mamma sapeva parlare tre lingue correttamente quando aveva vent'anni.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. English 'could speak (a language)' maps to the imperfect of sapere, not potere, because it's a skill ability.

reference_translations.text: La mamma sapeva parlare correttamente tre lingue quando aveva vent'anni.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: adverb placement before the object.

reference_translations.text: La mamma poteva parlare tre lingue correttamente quando aveva vent'anni.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: poteva parlare would mean 'was allowed / able to speak (in some situation)' — the permission/circumstance reading, not the skill reading. For language-skill ability, Italian uses sapere.

translation.reveal: could (skill, e.g. languages, swimming) - sapere; could (permission / circumstantial ability) - potere

explanation: English 'could' is ambiguous in a way Italian isn't. For a skill ability — speaking a language, swimming, playing an instrument — Italian uses sapere ('sapeva parlare tre lingue'). For permission or situation-dependent ability — being allowed to do something, being free to — Italian uses potere. Mum's language skills are the skill reading, so sapere. Saperva at this age (vent'anni) is a past skill-state, so imperfect.
examiner_note: English-speaker sleeper miss: defaulting to poteva because 'could' looks closer to potere. The negative reference flags it.

source_text: He insisted on paying the bill himself.

reference_translations.text: Ha voluto pagare il conto lui stesso.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. PP of volere = insisted on (got his way).

reference_translations.text: Ha insistito per pagare il conto lui.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'insistere per' is the explicit form; ha voluto carries the same meaning.

translation.reveal: to insist - PP of volere (ha voluto) / insistere per

explanation: English 'insisted on' maps to the PP of volere when the insistence prevailed. 'Ha voluto pagare il conto lui stesso' carries the strong-will, got-his-way reading specifically.

source_text: Volevo dirti la verità ma non ne ho avuto il coraggio.

reference_translations.text: I wanted to tell you the truth but I didn't have the courage.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect volevo = wanted (but didn't follow through). PP 'non ne ho avuto' for the discrete failure.

translation.reveal: volere - to want (volevo = wanted, didn't act)

explanation: Volevo (imperfect) = wanted (but didn't follow through). English 'wanted' captures the same ambiguity. The 'ma non ne ho avuto' clause makes the non-fulfilment explicit.

source_text: My grandmother always wanted me to become a teacher.

reference_translations.text: Mia nonna voleva sempre che facessi l'insegnante.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect voleva for the sustained wishing. Subjunctive facessi after volere che.

reference_translations.text: Mia nonna voleva sempre che io diventassi insegnante.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'diventassi' (imperfect subjunctive of diventare); 'insegnante' without 'l''.

explanation: Sustained past wishing (sempre) takes imperfect volere. Italian 'volere che + subjunctive' for 'want someone to do X'. The subordinate verb is in imperfect subjunctive (facessi or diventassi).
examiner_note: Triggers imperfect subjunctive in the subordinate, which crosses into territory beyond imperfect indicative. Acceptable since it's required for the natural translation.

source_text: I met my husband at university.

reference_translations.text: Ho conosciuto mio marito all'università.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. PP of conoscere = met for the first time.

reference_translations.text: Ho incontrato mio marito all'università.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'ho incontrato' (PP of incontrare = ran into). Acceptable but loses the 'got to know' nuance.

translation.reveal: to meet (and get to know) - PP of conoscere (ho conosciuto); to run into - PP of incontrare

explanation: English 'met (and got to know)' → PP of conoscere, the canonical first-meeting event reading. 'Ho conosciuto mio marito' is the textbook 'I met my husband'.

source_text: Conoscevo già il quartiere prima di trasferirmi qui.

reference_translations.text: I already knew the neighbourhood before I moved here.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect conoscevo = was acquainted with.

reference_translations.text: I was already familiar with the neighbourhood before moving here.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'familiar with' for conoscevo.

translation.reveal: conoscere - to know (conoscevo = was acquainted with)

explanation: Imperfect conoscevo = state of acquaintance with a place. English 'knew' or 'was familiar with' both work. The 'già prima di trasferirmi' (already before moving) reinforces the prior-state reading.

source_text: She had known the family since childhood.

reference_translations.text: Conosceva la famiglia fin dall'infanzia.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Imperfect conosceva for 'had known' (state of long acquaintance). 'Fin da' for 'since'.

reference_translations.text: Conosceva la famiglia da quando era bambina.
reference_translations.notes: Variant: 'da quando era bambina' (since she was a child) — paraphrase.

translation.reveal: had known (since) - imperfect of conoscere + fin da / da quando

explanation: English past perfect 'had known' for a sustained prior state maps to Italian imperfect conosceva. The 'since X' phrase fin dall'infanzia anchors the duration. Note the contrast with PP ha conosciuto (met for the first time), which doesn't fit.
